content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 11, 2018

Inder Khalsa, Attorney at Law
Richards Watson Gershon
44 Montgomery Street, Suite 3800
San Francisco, CA 94104

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-248

Dear Ms. Khalsa:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Cate Mayfield, Mill Valley History Room Librarian, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Government Code Section 1090. Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions. Note also that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request, and all pertinent facts relating to the request, to the Attorney General's Office and the Marin County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Is the City prohibited from entering future agreements with TimeWalk, a nonprofit, tax exempt 501 (c)(3) organization, if Ms. Mayfield becomes an uncompensated volunteer member of the TimeWalk Board of Directors?

2. Is Ms. Mayfield prohibited from participating in the making or administration of any future agreements with TimeWalk in her role as the City's librarian if she serves as an uncompensated volunteer member of the TimeWalk Board of Directors?

________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-248
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. The City and TimeWalk would not be prohibited from entering future agreements because Ms. Mayfield would be a volunteer board member of a non-profit organization.

2. No. Ms. Mayfield would not be prohibited from participating in the making or administration of any future agreements with TimeWalk in her role as the City's librarian if she served as an uncompensated volunteer member of the TimeWalk Board of Directors.

FACTS

You are the authorized representative of Mayfield and the City of Mill Valley (the "City"). Mayfield is employed as the History Room Librarian for the City of Mill Valley Public Library (the "Library").

Ms. Mayfield coordinates programs for the Library on local history. In part, she helped apply for, and now administers, a grant received from the California State Library's "Pitch an Idea" program. The grant funds are being used to develop the City's "Building History in 3D" program, which allows teenagers to develop their technological skills while creating 3D models of historic Mill Valley structures. The program began as an informal collaboration between the City and TimeWalk, a nonprofit, tax-exempt 503(c)(3) organization. TimeWalk's founder has volunteered to work with the City to create this program and develop a template for other libraries and communities to create similar 3D historical models. The informal collaboration involves no funds transferred from the City to TimeWalk.

TimeWalk is building an open-source project that will enable student-contributors to develop their technological skills and build historically accurate 3D models of their respective towns. Mayfield has been asked to serve on the Board of Directors for TimeWalk. If Ms. Mayfield becomes a member of the Board of Directors, then she would not receive any compensation for it.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 and the Act prohibit public officials from participating in decisions that affect matters in which they have financial interests. (See Section 87100 (c).) Neither the Act nor Section 1090 require public officials to resign from their positions when confronted with financial conflicts.

A. The Political Reform Act.

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to tell or influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Ms. Mayfield is a City employee and thus a public official under the Act. (Section 82048(a).)

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's

File No. A-17-248
Page No. 3

interests. (Section 87103.) Section 87103 of the Act lists several types of financial interests that can give rise to a conflict of interest, four of which are most relevant here:

• Source of Income – A public official has a financial interest in any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• Business Entity – A public official has a financial interest in any business entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Personal Finances – A public official has a financial interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

• Gift – A public official has a financial interest in any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty dollars or more in value, if the gift is provided or promised to the official within 12 months prior to a governmental decision. (Section 87103(e).)

Sources of Income and Gifts: As a volunteer Board member for TimeWalk, Ms. Mayfield would receive no compensation or reportable gifts in exchange for her services. Thus, TimeWalk is not an economic interest as a source of income under Section 87103(c) and (e).

Business Positions: Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise operated for a profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association." TimeWalk is a nonprofit tax exempt 501 (c)(3) organization, which is not a "business entity" under Section 87103(d).

Personal Finances. Next, we must determine whether participating in decisions about TimeWalk would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your personal finances under the "personal financial effects" rule. Regulation 18702.5(a) provides: "A personal financial effect means the financial effect of a governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision." In this case, there are no facts to suggest that any decisions will have any financial effect on Ms. Mayfield or her immediate family's personal finances.

Accordingly, Ms. Mayfield's volunteer service for TimeWalk would not be a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87103, nor would it give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act.

File No. A-17-248
Page No. 4

B.      Government Code Section 1090.

In addition to the Act, we must also determine whether Ms. Mayfield would have a
potentially disqualifying interest in a decision regarding future agreements between the City and
TimeWalk. Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. (People v. Honig (1996) 48
Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of whether the
terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633,
646-649).

In this case, there is no question that Ms. Mayfield is a public officer subject to the
provisions of Section 1090 and that any future agreement between the City and TimeWalk would
constitute a public contract for purposes of Section 1090. The determinative question is whether
Ms. Mayfield would have a financial interest prohibiting her from participating in the contract
under Section 1090 or the City from entering the contract, since TimeWalk is a nonprofit and
Ms. Mayfield would be an unpaid volunteer.

Sections 1091 and 1091.5 set forth various statutory categories of "remote interests" and
"noninterests" under Section 1090. If an official has only a "remote interest," the contract may be
made if (1) the official in question discloses his or her financial interest in the contract, (2) the
interest is noted in the public entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any
participation in the making of the contract. (Section 1091(a); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108
(2005); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) If a "noninterest" is present, the official in question
may participate in the contract and disclosure is not generally necessary. (City of Vernon v. Central
Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160
(2001).)

Section 1091.5(a), under "noninterests," includes in relevant part:

(8) That of a noncompensated officer of a nonprofit, tax-exempt
corporation, which, as one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of
the body or board or to which the body or board has a legal obligation to
give particular consideration, and provided further that this interest is noted
in its official records.

For purposes of this paragraph, an officer is "noncompensated" even though
he or she receives reimbursement from the nonprofit, tax-exempt
corporation for necessary travel and other actual expenses incurred in
performing the duties of his or her office.

You have indicated that TimeWalk is a tax-exempt organization and Ms. Mayfield would be
uncompensated. The question, therefore, is whether one of TimeWalk's primary purposes supports
the functions of the City. You state that "TimeWalk was created to showcase historical research and
support the creation of 3D models of local buildings, landmarks, and street scenes." The facts
provided show that TimeWalk's primary purposes support the functions of the City.

File No. A-17-248
Page No. 5

For the reasons discussed above, Ms. Mayfield has a "non-interest" in TimeWalk and is not
precluded by Section 1090 from participating in the making or administration of any future
agreements with TimeWalk in her role as the City's librarian or a volunteer member of the
TimeWalk Board of Directors. Moreover, the City is not precluded from entering future agreements
with TimeWalk because of Ms. Mayfield's volunteer service on TimeWalk's Board of Directors.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: John M. Feser Jr.
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JMF:jgl

sections.conclusion:
1. No. The City and TimeWalk would not be prohibited from entering future agreements 
because Ms. Mayfield would be a volunteer board member of a non-profit organization. 
2. No. Ms. Mayfield would not be prohibited from participating in the making or 
administration of any future agreements with Time Walk in her role as the City's librarian if 
she served as an uncompensated volunteer member of the TimeWalk Board of Directors.

sections.analysis:
Section 1090 and the Act prohibit public officials from participating in decisions that affect 
matters in which they have financial interests. (See Section 87100 (c).) Neither the Act nor Section 
1090 require public officials to resign from their positions when confronted with financial conflicts. 
A. 
The Political Reform Act. 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Ms. Mayfield is a City employee and thus a public official under the 
Act. (Section 82048(a).) 
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's  
interests. (Section 8 7103.) Section 8 7103 of the Act lists several types of financial interests that can 
give rise to a conflict of interest, four of which are most relevant here: 
• Source of Income -A public official has a financial interest in any source of 
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
• Business Entity - A public official has a financial interest in any business 
entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or 
holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• Personal Finances - A public official has a financial interest in his or her 
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is 
known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
• Gift-A public official has a financial interest in any donor of a gift or gifts 
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars or more in value, if the gift is provided 
or promised to the official within 12 months prior to a governmental decision. 
(Section 87103(e).) 
Sources of Income and Gifts: As a volunteer Board member for TimeWalk, Ms. Mayfield 
would receive no compensation or reportable gifts in exchange for her services. Thus, TimeWalk is 
not an economic interest as a source of income under Section 87103(c) and (e). 
Business Positions: Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or 
enterprise operated for a profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, 
business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association." TimeWalk is a nonprofit tax 
exempt 501 (c)(3) organization, which is not a "business entity" under Section 87103(d). 
Personal Finances. Next, we must detennine whether participating in decisions about 
Time Walk would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your personal finances under the 
"personal financial effects" rule. Regulation 18702.S(a) provides: "A personal financial effect 
means the financial effect of a governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or 
his or her immediate family. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's 
immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision." In 
this case, there are no facts to suggest that any decisions will have any financial effect on 
Ms. Mayfield or her immediate family's personal finances. 
Accordingly, Ms. Mayfield's volunteer service for Time Walk would not be a financial 
interest within the meaning of Section 87103, nor would it give rise to a conflict of interest under 
the Act. 


B. 
Government Code Section 1090.  
In addition to the Act, we must also detennine whether Ms. Mayfield would have a 
potentially disqualifying interest in a decision regarding future agreements between the City and 
Time Walk. Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. (People v. Honig (1996) 48 
Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of whether the 
tenns of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 
646-649). 
In this case, there is no question that Ms. Mayfield is a public officer subject to the 
provisions of Section 1090 and that any future agreement between the City and Time Walk would 
constitute a public contract for purposes of Section 1090. The determinative question is whether 
Ms. Mayfield would have a financial interest prohibiting her from participating in the contract 
under Section 1090 or the City from entering the contract, since TimeWalk is a nonprofit and 
Ms. Mayfield would be an unpaid volunteer. 
Sections 1091 and 1091.5 set forth various statutory categories of"remote interests" and 
"noninterests" under Section 1090. If an official has only a "remote interest," the contract may be 
made if (1) the official in question discloses his or her financial interest in the contract, (2) the 
interest is noted in the public entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any 
participation in the making of the contract. (Section 109l(a); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108 
(2005); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) If a "noninterest" is present, the official in question 
may participate in the contract and disclosure is not generally necessary. (City of Vernon v. Central 
Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 
{2001).) 
Section 1091.S{a), under "noninterests," includes in relevant part: 
(8) That of a noncompensated officer of a nonprofit, tax-exempt 
corporation, which, as one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of 
the body or board or to which the body or board has a legal obligation to 
give particular consideration, and provided further that this interest is noted 
in its official records. 
For purposes of this paragraph, an officer is "noncompensated" even though 
he or she receives reimbursement from the nonprofit, tax-exempt 
corporation for necessary travel and other actual expenses incurred in 
perfonning the duties of his or her office. 
You have indicated that TimeWalk is a tax-exempt organization and Ms. Mayfield would be 
uncompensated. The question, therefore, is whether one ofTimeWalk's primary purposes supports 
the functions of the City. You state that "Time Walk was created to showcase historical research and 
support the creation of 3D models of local buildings, landmarks, and street scenes." The facts 
provided show that TimeWalk's primary purposes support the functions of the City.  
For the reasons discussed above, Ms. Mayfield has a "non-interest" in TimeWalk and is not 
precluded by Section 1090 from participating in the making or administration of any future 
agreements with TimeWalk in her role as the City's librarian or a volunteer member of the 
TimeWalk Board of Directors. Moreover, the City is not precluded from entering future agreements
